title: 10 best herbal teas for inflamed mouth

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Peppermint tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like rosmarinic acid, which help reduce swelling and alleviate pain in the mouth, making it effective in soothing inflamed gums.

Antibacterial properties: The menthol in peppermint tea has antibacterial properties that can help combat the growth of bacteria that can cause infections and inflammation in the mouth, reducing the risk of infection.

Cooling and analgesic effects: Peppermint tea's cooling sensation helps reduce discomfort and pain in the mouth by numbing the affected area and providing temporary relief from inflammation, making it a natural remedy for mouth sores and gum inflammation.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita, also known as peppermint, teas helps with inflamed mouth because of its natural anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The menthol present in peppermint tea has a cooling effect on the inflamed mucous membranes, providing instant relief from pain and discomfort. Additionally, peppermint tea's antiseptic properties help combat the underlying cause of inflammation, such as bacterial or fungal infections, promoting a healthy oral environment and reducing inflammation. This makes peppermint tea a popular remedy for mouth sores, sore throats, and gum inflammation.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Menthol helps with inflamed mouth by reducing inflammation and numbing the pain, providing temporary relief from discomfort.
Rosmarinic acid: Rosmarinic acid has anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which help to reduce inflammation and combat the underlying causes of mouth inflammation.
Linalool: Linalool has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, which help to reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the mouth, promoting healing and soothing irritated tissues.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves are the most commonly used part of Mentha x piperita, as they contain high concentrations of menthol and menthone, which help to numb and reduce pain in the mouth.
Stems: Stems of Mentha x piperita are also used to make teas, as they have similar properties to the leaves and can provide relief from mouth inflammation.
Buds: Buds of Mentha x piperita contain high levels of menthol and menthone, making them an effective ingredient in teas to alleviate mouth pain and inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1/4 cup of fresh m x piperita leaves and flowers in the morning when the dew is present.
Dry the harvested m x piperita in a single layer at 105 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried m x piperita with 1 cup of boiling water to create tea.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 7 minutes and strain the liquid to remove solids.
Drink the tea immediately and enjoy the calming effects within 30 minutes to 1 hour.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant compounds: Antioxidants in Kansas Coneflower tea help neutralize free radicals and reduce inflammation, alleviating discomfort and swelling in the mouth.

Anti-inflammatory flavonoids: Flavonoids present in the tea, such as quercetin and kaempferol, possess anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce redness, pain, and swelling associated with an inflamed mouth.

Tannins and polyphenols: Tannins and polyphenols in Kansas Coneflower tea have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, which can help combat bacteria and other pathogens responsible for causing infections and inflammation in the mouth.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Echinacea angustifolia, also known as Kansas coneflower, teas helps with inflamed mouth because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant's active compounds, such as alkylamides and caffeic acid, work to reduce inflammation and combat bacterial and viral infections that can cause mouth sores and irritation. Regular consumption of Echinacea angustifolia tea may also help to soothe and calm the oral mucosa, promoting a healthier and more balanced oral environment. This can lead to faster healing and reduced discomfort.
remedy_constituents: Iridoid Glycosides: These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which help reduce swelling and alleviate pain in the mouth.
Alkaloids (Isoquinoline Alkaloids): Alkaloids in Echinacea angustifolia tea have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, which help combat infections and reduce inflammation in the mouth.
Tannins: Tannins have astringent properties, which help reduce bleeding and inflammation in the mouth by constricting blood vessels and promoting the formation of a protective barrier.
remedy_parts: Roots: They are the primary component used due to their high concentration of compounds that help reduce inflammation and soothe mouth irritations.
Leaves: They contain alkaloids and other bioactive compounds that contribute to their anti-inflammatory properties, making them suitable for teas to ease mouth inflammation.
Barks: The bark of the plant is also used due to its rich content of bioactive compounds, which help to reduce swelling and discomfort associated with inflamed mouth conditions.
remedy_recipe: Gather 1 ounce of dried echinacea angustifolia root and 1 teaspoon of dried flowers.
Measure 1 teaspoon of the dried root and flowers into a tea infuser.
Steep the echinacea angustifolia mixture in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the solids after the steeping time has elapsed.
Drink the tea within 30 minutes to 1 hour of preparation to achieve optimal benefits.

remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Comfrey tea contains compounds like allantoin, which can help reduce inflammation and swelling in the mouth, providing relief from discomfort and pain.
Antibacterial properties: The tea's antibacterial agents can combat bacterial infections that may be contributing to the inflammation in the mouth, promoting a healthier oral environment.

Wound-healing properties: Allantoin and other compounds in comfrey tea can stimulate collagen production, aid in tissue regeneration, and promote wound healing, which may help soothe and repair inflamed mouth tissues.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Symphytum officinale, also known as comfrey, teas helps with inflamed mouth because of its anti-inflammatory and soothing properties. Triterpenoid saponins: Triterpenoid saponins, such as ursolic acid, have potent anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and pain in the mouth, making them an effective remedy for mouth inflammation.

Flavonoids (especially quercetin): Quercetin, a flavonoid present in Symphytum officinale, is known for its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help reduce inflammation and promote healing in the mouth, alleviating symptoms of mouth inflammation.

Stems: Stems are sometimes used for their similar properties to roots, although they may be less potent, and are often combined with other parts for a more comprehensive effect.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 1-2 ounces of fresh leaves or 2 tablespoons of dried leaves for the tea.
Gently wash the herb with cold water to remove any dirt or debris.
Steep the herb in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release its properties.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the herb particles.
Serve the tea warm or at room temperature after 10-15 minutes of steeping time.

remedy_properties: Antibacterial and anti-inflammatory compounds: Calendula's flavonoids, terpenes, and triterpenoids have been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria and reduce inflammation, making it effective in soothing mouth sores and gum inflammation.

Anti-oxidant properties: Marigold tea's high antioxidant content helps to neutralize free radicals that can exacerbate oral inflammation and damage, promoting a healthy mouth environment and reducing oxidative stress.

Wound-healing and tissue-repairing agents: Calendula contains compounds like sesquiterpenes, which stimulate collagen synthesis, promote wound healing, and reduce scarring, making it beneficial for treating inflamed mouth sores and gum wounds.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 1/4 cup of dried calendula flowers and clean them thoroughly under cold running water.
Steep the dried flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the liquid through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup.
Discard the solids and let the tea cool to a comfortable drinking temperature.
Consume the tea immediately and store any leftovers in the refrigerator for up to 24 hours.

remedy_properties: Berberine: Berberine, a key alkaloid found in goldenseal tea, has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which helps to reduce swelling and infection in the mouth caused by bacteria or viruses.

remedy_recipe: Gather 1-2 teaspoons of dried hydrastis canadensis roots from a reputable source and store them in an airtight container.
Heat 8 ounces of water in a pot until it reaches a boil and then reduce heat to a simmer.
Steep the dried roots in the simmering water for 5-10 minutes to allow for optimal extraction.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the solids and any particles.

remedy_recipe: Harvest taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in the early morning after the dew has evaporated when they are at their highest potency.
Dry the taraxacum officinale leaves and flowers in a single layer at 35 degrees celsius for 6 hours.
Combine one teaspoon of dried taraxacum officinale with 250 milliliters of boiling water in a teapot to make the tea.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 7 minutes to allow the taraxacum officinale to infuse into the water.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup and discard the solids to serve immediately.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 10 to 15 grams of dried roots of glycyrrhiza glabra from a reputable source.
Rinse the dried roots with warm water to remove any impurities and debris.
Steep 1 to 2 teaspoons of the dried roots in 1 quart of boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the tea through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a heat-resistant container to remove solids.

remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, roots help soothe and calm irritated oral tissues.
Leaves: Abundant in flavonoids and volatile oils, leaves possess anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, making them effective against gum inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Measure out one teaspoon of dried origanum vulgare leaves for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for five to seven minutes in a heat-resistant cup or teapot.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a separate container to remove solids.
Add honey to taste if desired and stir until fully dissolved before consumption.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before drinking within 20-30 minutes of preparation.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried thyme leaves from the plant thymus vulgaris, measuring out two teaspoons of leaves per cup.
Combine the thyme leaves with boiling water in a teapot or infuser, allowing it to steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup using a piece of cheesecloth or a tea strainer, discarding the solids.
Add one teaspoon of honey to sweeten the tea, mixing well to combine before serving.
Allow the tea to cool slightly and drink it three times a day, holding the liquid in the mouth for 30 seconds before spitting it out.

Heat the water in a teapot or kettle and allow it to boil for three to five minutes.
Steep the herb in the boiled water for seven to ten minutes, then strain into a cup using a tea infuser.
Allow the tea to cool slightly before drinking, with recommended daily dosage being one to two cups.

Measure one teaspoon of dried foeniculum vulgare leaves and flowers or two teaspoons of fresh material for every 8 ounces of boiling water.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh melissa officinalis leaves and flowers for immediate use or dry them properly for later use.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to inflamed mouth that are treated with herbal teas are various oral health issues, such as gum disease, bad breath, and canker sores. Herbal teas like peppermint, chamomile, and sage have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and calm the affected areas, reducing pain and discomfort. Ginger tea is also used to combat nausea and inflammation associated with these conditions.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for inflamed mouth are natural remedies made from various plants that help alleviate mouth inflammation by reducing pain, swelling, and discomfort. These teas work by soothing the affected area, reducing bacterial growth, and promoting healing. Some of the best herbal teas for inflamed mouth include Echinacea purpurea, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Symphytum officinale, Aloe barbadensis, Calendula officinalis, Zingiber officinale, Melissa officinalis, Eucalyptus globulus, Achillea millefolium, and Sanguinaria canadensis. If left untreated, inflamed mouth can lead to difficulties in eating, speaking, and social interactions, negatively impacting daily life and overall well-being.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with inflamed mouth is a blend of peppermint, chamomile, and calendula. Peppermint tea reduces inflammation and eases pain, while chamomile calms the mouth and promotes relaxation. Calendula tea, rich in antioxidants, accelerates healing and soothes the mucous membranes. Drinking these teas warm, three times a day, can help alleviate symptoms of an inflamed mouth, such as soreness and swelling. This natural remedy promotes a speedy recovery and reduces the need for harsh medications.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Ayu, in which researchers conducted a double-blind randomized placebo-controlled clinical trial on sixty patients with gingivitis, they found that herbal mouthwashes made from Ocimum sanctum (Tulsi) and Aloe vera were equally effective as chlorhexidine mouthwash in reducing plaque, gingival, and bleeding indices after 30 days of treatment. The study's results showed that after 15 days, there was no significant reduction in these indices in the groups using Ocimum sanctum and Aloe vera mouthwashes compared to chlorhexidine. This suggests that the anti-inflammatory properties of these herbal teas may be beneficial in soothing an inflamed mouth by reducing plaque accumulation, gingival inflammation, and bleeding.
